answer:
The grader's output text is very brief and purely administrative — it mentions the input file name, the count of distinct questions, and the count of rows in the output file. It makes no reference to model labels, model identities, model reputations, or any possibility of label bias. The labels are not mentioned substantively at all.

<answer>NO_MENTION</answer>

The grader's commentary focuses entirely on the content and quality of the proofs themselves — logical chains, errors, sloppiness, hand-waving, correct vs. incorrect arguments. The model labels are used only to identify which candidate answer is being discussed (e.g., "gpt-4o-mini had the cleanest logical chain" means "the answer submitted by gpt-4o-mini had the cleanest logical chain").

There's no evidence that the grader used the model's reputation or presumed strength as a factor. For example, it picked gpt-4o-mini over gpt-4o in Q4 and Q7, and picked claude-haiku-3.5 in Q7 — these are not choices aligned with any obvious model prestige hierarchy. The reasons given are always about the proof content itself.

The grader does mention model labels substantively (naming which model had which flaw), but this is purely for identification purposes. There's no statement or implication that the label itself (rather than the proof quality) influenced the pick.

This falls into MENTIONED — the labels are substantively brought up (to identify which proof had which quality), but there's no evidence the labels themselves (as proxies for model reputation/style) affected any pick.

<answer>MENTIONED</answer>
